frameworks.name: bounded accumulation of evidence
frameworks.one_line: log-likelihood-ratio accumulation to a bound governs both choice and strategy switches

frameworks.name: hierarchical decision-making
frameworks.one_line: high-level strategy and trial-level choice interact across timescales

frameworks.name: active inference
frameworks.one_line: disambiguating negative feedback as bad information vs bad strategy

frameworks.name: signal-detection theory
frameworks.one_line: expected accuracy on perceptual choices weights feedback evidence

core_question: How do subjects integrate outcomes and expected accuracy across choices to decide when to switch a higher-level decision strategy?
key_assumptions: expected accuracy of a choice modulates the weight given to feedback
switch evidence is accumulated as log-likelihood ratio across trials
strategy is updated when accumulated evidence reaches a bound
feedback is ambiguous between limited information and wrong strategy
manipulations: invisible unpredictable switches of correct stimulus-response mapping every few trials
key_findings: subjects treat negative feedback as evidence for a switch
feedback is weighted by the subject's expected accuracy on that trial
switch evidence accumulates across trials until a bound triggers strategy update
a single computational framework quantitatively captures all behavioral aspects
interpretations: bounded evidence accumulation underlies both the choice and the strategy that governs the choice
expected accuracy is the key link between hierarchical levels of decision-making
